title: What is a Decision Tree?
concept: A decision tree is a flowchart-like model that makes predictions by asking a series of yes/no questions about features to route data down branches to a final answer.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What makes the R-squared (R²) metric useful in evaluation?

options.text: It scores models on a standardized scale from 0 to 1, comparing them to a naive baseline.
It measures errors in the exact original units of the target column.
It is only used to measure classification accuracy.

exercises.explain: Correct! R-squared provides a normalized 0 to 1 score indicating what percentage of the variance is explained, unlike distance-based error metrics.

cards.title: Choose Your Own Adventure
cards.body: Imagine playing a game of 20 Questions. You ask: 'Is it an animal?' If yes, you ask: 'Does it fly?' If no, you ask: 'Does it bark?' Step by step, your questions split the possibilities until you guess 'Dog'. That flow of questions is exactly how a decision tree thinks.

cards.title: Nodes, Branches, and Leaves
cards.body: A decision tree starts at a root node, splits into branches based on features, and ends at leaf nodes. A root node is the starting question. Branches represent the answers (e.g., Yes/No). Leaf nodes are the final predictions (e.g., 'Spam' or 'Not Spam').

cards.title: Road signs on a highway
cards.body: Think of a decision tree like a series of forks in a highway. At each fork, a road sign asks a question about your destination. If your car meets the condition (e.g., weight > 2 tons), you turn right; if not, you turn left. You keep turning until you land in a parking lot (a leaf node) representing your final category.

cards.title: The Email Router
cards.body: A simple tree decides if an email is urgent: 1. Does the subject contain 'urgent'? Yes -> 2. Is it from your boss? Yes -> Leaf: 'Immediate Alert'. No -> Leaf: 'Inbox'. If the first answer is No -> Leaf: 'Inbox'. Each email travels down the branches to its proper classification.

cards.title: Decision Trees aren't black boxes
cards.body: Many people believe machine learning models are impossible to understand. Decision trees are actually interpretable models. You can print out the exact list of yes/no questions the model learned and follow them yourself, making it clear why a prediction was made.

cards.title: The Structure of a Tree
cards.body: How data routes from the root to the leaf:

left.text:
The decision points.
Contain questions about features.
e.g. 'Is temperature > 90°?'

right.text:
The endpoints.
Contain the final predictions.
e.g. Classify as 'Hot Day'

exercises.prompt: In a decision tree, what does a leaf node represent?

options.text: The final prediction or output category of the model.
The first question the model asks at the very top.
A feature column that has been scaled using standard deviation.
The residual error of the training loss function.

exercises.explain: Correct! Leaf nodes are the endpoints of the tree branches where the final prediction is returned.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Decision trees are considered 'interpretable' because you can easily trace the exact yes/no questions they use to make a guess.
exercises.explain: True! Tracing a decision tree is as simple as following a flow chart, making it easy to explain its reasoning.

exercises.sentence: A decision tree starts at the ___ node and splits into branches until it reaches a leaf node.
exercises.explain: The root node sits at the very top of the tree, representing the first question asked.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a decision tree model in action?

options.text: An app routes a loan applicant based on: Income > $50k? (Yes) -> Credit score > 700? (Yes) -> Predict: Approve.
An app multiplies income by 0.5 and credit score by 0.8 to calculate a single credit score number.
An app clusters credit scores using distance centroids.

exercises.explain: Correct! Sequential yes/no conditional splits to reach a final classification is a decision tree.

exercises.prompt: You build a model to sort fruits. The model checks: 'Is color yellow?' Yes -> 'Is shape long?' Yes -> Predict: 'Banana'. What represents the root node, and what represents a leaf node?

options.text: Root node: 'Is color yellow?'; Leaf node: 'Banana'
Root node: 'Banana'; Leaf node: 'Is shape long?'
Root node: 'Is shape long?'; Leaf node: 'Is color yellow?'

exercises.explain: Exactly! The root node is the very first check ('Is color yellow?'). The leaf node is the final prediction returned at the end of the path ('Banana'). Now let's learn how a tree decides which question to ask first!
